raw_case:
Hyperthyroidism and fulminant myocarditis in an adolescent with iodine-induced hyperthyroidism: A case report
This paper describes the case of a 15-year-old male patient who exhibited chest distress and pain following an upper respiratory tract infection. Upon admission, the patient exhibited elevated levels of myocardial enzymes and troponin I. Electrocardiography revealed an acute inferior myocardial infarction. Coronary computed tomography angiography ruled out coronary stenosis; however, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging revealed myocardial congestion and edema. Subsequent examinations revealed increased thyroid hormone levels and decreased thyroid-stimulating hormone levels, which is consistent with the manifestations of hyperthyroidism. The patient was diagnosed with acute fulminant myocarditis accompanied by iodine-induced hyperthyroidism. The patient received no pharmacologic treatment for hyperthyroidism but was prescribed a low-iodine diet, medication, and supportive treatment. His myocardial enzymes and troponin I levels gradually decreased, his electrocardiography and echocardiography results improved, and his cardiac function returned to normal. The patient was discharged and advised to maintain a low-iodine diet. At the 6-month follow-up, the patient’s thyroid function and cardiac indicators had returned to normal. This case demonstrates that myocardial injury can result from myocarditis and iodine-induced hyperthyroidism and highlights the importance of closely monitoring and assessing thyroid function during treatment.

Introduction

Myocarditis is a complex inflammatory disease usually resulting from viral infections that selectively involve the myocardium or autoimmune processes.
1
 Fulminant myocarditis (FM) is a rare syndrome characterized by sudden and severe diffuse cardiac inflammation that often leads to cardiogenic shock, ventricular arrhythmias, or death from multi-organ system failure.2,3 Thyroid dysfunction is usually induced by similar underlying mechanisms. In particular, hyperthyroidism has significant and well-recognized effects on the cardiovascular system at the molecular and circulatory levels. Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism (IIH) often develops after exposure to large amounts of iodine or long-term use of amiodarone.
4
 IIH and myocarditis are not linked by a direct cause-and-effect relationship; however, hyperthyroidism may have certain effects on the heart that indirectly affect the course and symptoms of myocarditis. Here, we report a rare case of FM accompanied by IIH.

Case presentation

A 15-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital due to “chest distress and pain for 9 hours.” The patient had a history of staying up late and presented with symptoms of upper respiratory tract infection, such as nasal discharge and sore throat, 2 weeks prior. Vital signs were stable upon admission, and physical examination revealed no significant irregularities. Emergency laboratory tests showed creatine kinase isoenzyme (CK-MB) 69.3 U/L and cardiac troponin I (cTnI) 13.44 ng/mL. Electrocardiography (ECG) revealed ST-segment elevation of 0.3–0.6 mV in leads II, III, aVF, and V4–V6, which is indicative of acute inferior myocardial infarction. Thus, the patient was diagnosed with acute inferior–posterior wall myocardial infarction. An emergency coronary computed tomography angiography (CTA) revealed normal coronary development, a smooth endothelium, and no stenosis. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging revealed high signal areas in the inferior wall of the left ventricle on T2-weighted imaging and focal enhancement on late gadolinium-enhanced imaging. Since the patient did not meet the criteria for acute inferior–posterior wall myocardial infarction, the diagnosis was revised to acute FM and cardiac catheterization was not performed. Treatment prescribed by the consultants that were brought on board to take care of the patient included bed rest, fosinopril and metoprolol, high-dose vitamin C, coenzyme Q10, 1,6-fructose diphosphate for myocardial nutrition support, and symptomatic relief. After 12 h, repeat laboratory examination showed lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 502.7 U/L, LDH1 221.2 U/L; creatine kinase (CK) >2000 U/L; CK-MB 279 U/L; cTnI >20.64 ng/mL; α-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase (αHBDH) 499.3 U/L; aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 257.2 U/L; and hypersensitive C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) >10 mg/mL. The eosinophil count was 0.2 cells/μL (normal range, 0.02–0.5 cells/μL). No evidence of influenza, parainfluenza, or COVID-19 was observed in the conducted examinations.On the first day of admission, a re-examination of CK, CK-MB, cTnI, and BNP levels revealed a continued increase, with BNP at 265.1 pg/mL. A thyroid panel revealed thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) 0.05 μIU/mL (reference range, 0.55–4.78 μIU/mL); triiodothyronine (T3) 5.61 nmol/L (reference range, 0.92–2.79 nmol/L); thyroxine (T4) 178.38 nmol/L (reference range, 58.1–140.6 nmol/L); free triiodothyronine (FT3) 21.14 pmol/L (reference range, 3.5–6.5 pmol/L); free thyroxine (FT4) 29.9 pmol/L (reference range, 11.5–22.5 pmol/L); anti-thyroglobulin antibody (TGAb) 1 IU/L (reference range, 0–1.75 IU/L); anti-thyroid peroxidase (TPOAb) 0.5 IU/L (reference range, 0–1.75 IU/L); and thyroglobulin (Tg) 2.29 ng/mL. Thyroid ultrasound showed no significant abnormalities. Echocardiography revealed a dilated left ventricular end-systolic diameter with reduced wall motion, mild regurgitation in the mitral, tricuspid, and aortic valves, and reduced left ventricular systolic function (ejection fraction (EF) 46%, fractional shortening (FS) 23%). A low-iodine diet was prescribed, and anti-thyroid medication was temporarily withheld (Tables 1 and 2).On the third day of hospitalization, a re-examination of CK, CK-MB, CTnI, hs-CRP, and BNP showed decreased levels, while thyroid function test indicators remained abnormal with no significant changes. ECG revealed a reduction in the range of ST-segment elevation in the related leads, while echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular systolic function (EF 55%, FS 33%) (Tables 1 and 2).On the seventh day of hospitalization, the patient reported no discomfort. Re-examination of indicators showed LDH 138.6 U/L; LDH1 86.7 U/L; CK 180.5 U/L; CK-MB 24 U/L; CTnI 1.25 ng/mL; αHBDH 176.2 U/L; AST 36.4 U/L; hs-CRP 1.08 mg/mL; and BNP 65.1 ng/mL. The repeat thyroid panel showed TSH 0.045 μIU/mL; T3 5.50 nmol/L; T4 158.72 nmol/L; FT3 17.60 pmol/L; FT4 26.34 pmol/L; TGAb 1.30 IU/L; TPOAb 0.63 IU/L; and Tg 2.37 ng/mL. The repeat ECG indicated a sinus rhythm (Tables 1 and 2).The patient’s condition improved with treatment, and he was discharged on the 10th day with instructions to adhere to a low-iodine diet. Follow-up at the outpatient clinic 3 months later showed normal liver function and myocardial enzyme and troponin I levels. Thyroid function tests had significantly improved. At the 6-month follow-up to date, the patient’s thyroid function had returned to normal, with no adverse events reported.

Discussion

Acute myocarditis is most commonly viral in nature. In the present case, the adolescent patient had developed an upper respiratory tract infection, which led to acute myocarditis. The patient exhibited persistent chest pain and ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads, along with abnormally high myocardial enzymes and troponin I levels. The absence of obstructive lesions on coronary CTA and myocardial edema and injury in the left ventricular region on cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed the diagnosis of acute FM.The changes in the patient’s ECG ST-segment indicated myocardial edema and injury, which worsened cell membrane permeability, caused the accumulation of biological products and reduced the energy supply and oxygenation of myocardial tissue.
5
 With treatment, the patient’s ECG ST-segment returned to normal, the cardiac ventricular structure improved, valvular regurgitation diminished, and normal cardiac function was restored.The patient did not exhibit symptoms of thyroid toxicity, such as fever, excessive sweating, or a significantly increased heart rate (>120 beats per minute). Thyroid examination and ultrasound showed no goiter, and no fine hand tremor was observed. Considering the patient’s age, exposure to iodinated contrast media (ICM) during the coronary CTA may have disrupted his thyroid hormone autoregulation, resulting in destructive thyroiditis associated with subsequent sporadic hyperthyroidism. Hyperthyroidism is characterized by increased T3 and T4 levels and decreased TSH levels. Furthermore, these symptoms are consistent with those of the Jod–Basedow phenomenon, which can lead to thyroid storm and severe cardiac complications, including arrhythmias and heart failure.6–9 A previous report described the case of an elderly female patient who developed a Jod–Basedow phenomenon with subsequent atrial fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia after cervical CT imaging with iodinated contrast. Therefore, a comprehensive evaluation of a patient’s medical history can elucidate the correlation between cardiac and thyroid events and identify potential risk factors.
7
Acute myocarditis and hyperthyroidism typically occur in young, otherwise healthy patients. Autoimmunity is central to the pathogenesis of both, with thyroid dysfunction rarely associated with myocarditis. The diagnosis of myocarditis is based on magnetic resonance edema and troponin as a sign of myocardial necrosis. The patient did not undergo endomyocardial biopsy, which is the gold standard for diagnosing myocarditis. In its absence, we do not know the histotype of inflammation associated with hyperthyroidism. Is it an autoimmune myocarditis? Giant cell myocarditis? Lymphocytic myocarditis?
2
 Thyrotoxicosis often leads to cardiac consequences, and the prevalence of thyroid function abnormalities is related to that of myocarditis. Acute myocarditis is an autoimmune pathological process, often triggered by viral infections, which may be a risk factor for the development of IIH. Therefore, establishing a correlation between symptoms and exposure to ICM or other iodinated substances is crucial in obtaining an accurate diagnosis and providing suitable treatment.

Conclusion

This case demonstrated that myocarditis accompanied by IIH can be managed with a low-iodine diet, the use of β-adrenergic receptor blocker metoprolol, and close monitoring of thyroid function with regular follow-up assessments without requiring anti-hyperthyroid treatment and ECMO implantation. This treatment course showed good patient prognosis and complied with the requirements of relevant guidelines.This case report describes a rare case of a juvenile patient with FM complicated by IIH. The limitations of the study were the lack of thyroid nuclear imaging and myocardial biopsy evaluation, the latter of which is the gold standard for myocarditis diagnosis. Nevertheless, the current case provides valuable insights into the complex interplay among myocarditis, hyperthyroidism, and ICM exposure in the pediatric population.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:**
  - Age: 15-year-old male
  - Presentation: Admitted due to “chest distress and pain for 9 hours.”

- **Chief Complaint:** Chest distress and pain.

- **History of Present Illness:** 
  - Symptoms of upper respiratory tract infection (nasal discharge and sore throat) two weeks prior to admission.
  - History of late-night awake periods.

- **Past Medical History:** None mentioned.

- **Personal History:** None mentioned.

- **Family History:** None mentioned.

- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Vital signs: Stable.
  - No significant abnormalities noted.

- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Laboratory:
    - Elevated myocardial enzymes and troponin I (CK-MB 69.3 U/L, cTnI 13.44 ng/mL on admission).
    - Abnormal thyroid function tests: TSH 0.05 μIU/mL (low), T3 5.61 nmol/L (high), T4 178.38 nmol/L (high), FT3 21.14 pmol/L (high), FT4 29.9 pmol/L (high).
    - Inflammatory markers elevated: hs-CRP >10 mg/mL.
  - Imaging:
    - ECG: ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF, and V4–V6 (indicative of acute inferior myocardial infarction, but later revised).
    - Coronary CTA: No stenosis, normal coronary development.
    - Cardiac MRI: Myocardial congestion, edema, and focal enhancement.
    - Echocardiography: Reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF 46%), dilated left ventricular end-systolic diameter, mild valvular regurgitation.
    - Thyroid ultrasound: No significant abnormalities.

- **Treatment and Course:**
  - Initial treatment: Bed rest, fosinopril, metoprolol, high-dose vitamin C, coenzyme Q10, 1,6-fructose diphosphate, and supportive therapy.
  - Low-iodine diet prescribed; anti-thyroid medications withheld temporarily.
  - Improvement observed: Decline in myocardial enzyme levels, normalization of ECG ST-segment elevation, improved cardiac function (EF improved to 55% within 3 days).
  - Discharged on day 10 with instructions for follow-up care.

- **Outcomes:**
  - At 6-month follow-up, thyroid and cardiac function had returned to normal, with no adverse events reported.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Acute inferior-posterior myocardial infarction**:
   - **Included initially**: ST-segment elevation on ECG and elevated troponin I levels supported this diagnosis.
   - **Excluded**: Coronary CTA revealed no stenosis or coronary abnormalities; diagnosis revised to acute fulminant myocarditis.

2. **Acute fulminant myocarditis**:
   - **Included**: Confirmed by cardiac MRI (myocardial edema and injury in the left ventricle), elevated myocardial biomarkers, and clinical presentation following a recent viral illness.
   - **Rationale**: Myocardial injury and inflammation were evident. Coronary ischemia was ruled out by CTA.

3. **Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism (IIH)**:
   - **Considered based on findings**: Thyrotoxicosis (elevated T3 and T4, suppressed TSH levels) and recent exposure to iodinated contrast media (used during coronary CTA).
   - Symptoms consistent with the Jod–Basedow phenomenon, likely triggered by contrast-mediated thyroid hormone dysregulation.
   - **Included**: Laboratory findings confirmed hyperthyroidism without symptoms of thyroid toxicity.

4. **Autoimmune thyroid disease or alternate thyrotoxicosis**:
   - **Excluded**: Absence of goiter, normal thyroid ultrasound, and no clinical findings suggestive of autoimmune thyroid disorders or thyroid storm.

5. **Other causes of myocarditis**:
   - Eosinophilic myocarditis, giant cell myocarditis, and other histotypes were not definitively ruled out but were considered unlikely due to lack of myocardial biopsy and specific corroborative findings.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with acute fulminant myocarditis accompanied by iodine-induced hyperthyroidism. The myocarditis was likely triggered by a recent viral infection, while hyperthyroidism was attributed to iodinated contrast exposure during coronary CTA (Jod–Basedow phenomenon). Both conditions improved with supportive care and a low-iodine diet.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Acute fulminant myocarditis with iodine-induced hyperthyroidism.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:**
  - Age: 15-year-old male
  - Presentation: Admitted due to “chest distress and pain for 9 hours.”

- **Chief Complaint:** Chest distress and pain.

- **History of Present Illness:** 
  - Symptoms of upper respiratory tract infection (nasal discharge and sore throat) two weeks prior to admission.
  - History of late-night awake periods.

- **Past Medical History:** None mentioned.

- **Personal History:** None mentioned.

- **Family History:** None mentioned.

- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Vital signs: Stable.
  - No significant abnormalities noted.

- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Laboratory:
    - Elevated myocardial enzymes and troponin I (CK-MB 69.3 U/L, cTnI 13.44 ng/mL on admission).
    - Abnormal thyroid function tests: TSH 0.05 μIU/mL (low), T3 5.61 nmol/L (high), T4 178.38 nmol/L (high), FT3 21.14 pmol/L (high), FT4 29.9 pmol/L (high).
    - Inflammatory markers elevated: hs-CRP >10 mg/mL.
  - Imaging:
    - ECG: ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF, and V4–V6 (indicative of acute inferior myocardial infarction, but later revised).
    - Coronary CTA: No stenosis, normal coronary development.
    - Cardiac MRI: Myocardial congestion, edema, and focal enhancement.
    - Echocardiography: Reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF 46%), dilated left ventricular end-systolic diameter, mild valvular regurgitation.
    - Thyroid ultrasound: No significant abnormalities.

- **Treatment and Course:**
  - Initial treatment: Bed rest, fosinopril, metoprolol, high-dose vitamin C, coenzyme Q10, 1,6-fructose diphosphate, and supportive therapy.
  - Low-iodine diet prescribed; anti-thyroid medications withheld temporarily.
  - Improvement observed: Decline in myocardial enzyme levels, normalization of ECG ST-segment elevation, improved cardiac function (EF improved to 55% within 3 days).
  - Discharged on day 10 with instructions for follow-up care.

- **Outcomes:**
  - At 6-month follow-up, thyroid and cardiac function had returned to normal, with no adverse events reported.

---

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Resoning:
The patient is a 15-year-old male presenting with chest distress and pain for 9 hours, which is an alarming symptom in this age group. Initial investigations revealed elevated myocardial enzymes (CK-MB and troponin I) and ECG changes suggestive of acute myocardial infarction (MI). This immediately raised suspicion for an acute cardiac event.

However, the patient's history also revealed recent symptoms of an upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) two weeks prior, which is a known trigger for inflammatory cardiac conditions like myocarditis. Importantly, the ancillary tests unveiled profoundly abnormal thyroid function tests, showing suppressed TSH and significantly elevated thyroid hormones (T3, T4, FT3, FT4), indicative of severe hyperthyroidism. Elevated hs-CRP also suggested an inflammatory process.

Crucially, the coronary computed tomography angiography (CTA) ruled out significant coronary artery stenosis or anomalies, effectively excluding atherosclerotic coronary artery disease or congenital coronary abnormalities as the cause of the myocardial injury. The cardiac MRI showed myocardial congestion and edema with focal enhancement, findings more consistent with inflammatory or acute ischemic injury not necessarily due to blockage. Echocardiography revealed reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF 46%) and dilation, indicating cardiac dysfunction.

The key lies in correlating the hyperthyroidism with the cardiac manifestations. Severe hyperthyroidism, particularly thyrotoxic crisis (thyroid storm), can lead to significant cardiovascular complications including palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, arrhythmias, heart failure, and reversible myocardial dysfunction (thyrotoxic cardiomyopathy). The increased metabolic demand, direct toxic effects of excess thyroid hormone on myocytes, and potential microvascular dysfunction can lead to elevated myocardial enzymes and ECG changes that mimic acute MI. The URTI could have precipitated the hyperthyroid decompensation or crisis.

The patient's rapid improvement with supportive therapy, including cardiac medications and supplements, and the normalization of myocardial enzymes, ECG ST-segment elevation, and improved cardiac function (EF to 55% within 3 days) strongly suggest a reversible cause of myocardial injury rather than irreversible ischemic necrosis from coronary occlusion. The subsequent normalization of both thyroid and cardiac function at the 6-month follow-up further solidifies the diagnosis of a reversible cardiac condition driven by severe hyperthyroidism. The initial ECG findings, initially interpreted as MI, were likely atypical manifestations of the hyperthyroid state or associated inflammation.

### Answer:
Thyrotoxic cardiomyopathy secondary to severe hyperthyroidism

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 15-year-old male presenting with chest distress and pain for 9 hours, which is an alarming symptom in this age group. Initial investigations revealed elevated myocardial enzymes (CK-MB and troponin I) and ECG changes suggestive of acute myocardial infarction (MI). This immediately raised suspicion for an acute cardiac event.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Chest pain in adolescents, Elevated myocardial enzymes, CK-MB, Troponin I, Acute myocardial infarction in youth, ECG changes in MI, Causes of acute cardiac events in teenagers.
judgment_path.info:
Two athletic male adolescents presented with acute chest pain, elevated troponin and CK-MB levels, and focal ST elevation on ECG, initially suggesting myocardial infarction. However, further evaluation revealed myopericarditis as the underlying cause. This case highlights that while elevated cardiac enzymes are typically indicative of myocardial infarction, they can also be elevated due to other conditions such as myopericarditis, pulmonary embolism, and acute rheumatic fever. Thus, a careful differential diagnosis is essential for patients presenting with chest pain and elevated cardiac enzymes.

The evaluation of adolescents (ages 16-18) presenting with chest pain, elevated cardiac enzymes, and abnormal electrocardiograms (ECGs) poses significant diagnostic challenges, as myocardial infarction is rare in this group. A retrospective review identified nine such adolescents, all exhibiting ischemic ECG changes but without thrombotic coronary occlusion. It's crucial to consider alternative diagnoses, especially in patients without prior cardiac risk factors. Diagnostic imaging, particularly magnetic resonance imaging, is vital for differentiating between focal myocarditis and myocardial infarction. Until a definitive diagnosis is established, thrombolytic therapy or anticoagulation should be avoided.

Chest pain in children is a common complaint that often results in extensive cardiac evaluations. A study analyzed data from 7,251 patients under 18 years old, revealing that only 8% of chest pain cases had a cardiac origin, primarily due to conditions like myocarditis. The most prevalent concomitant symptom in cardiac cases was chest tightness, while many non-cardiac cases had no associated symptoms. Diagnostic criteria for targeted myocardial enzyme testing were established, emphasizing the importance of thorough medical history, physical examination, and ECG findings to effectively distinguish between cardiac and non-cardiac causes. This approach aims to reduce unnecessary testing and anxiety for the families while ensuring proper diagnosis and management.

Elevated hs-CRP also suggested an inflammatory process.

Echocardiography revealed reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF 46%) and dilation, indicating cardiac dysfunction.

judgment_path.info: 冠状动脉疾病（CAD）是由于冠状动脉的狭窄或阻塞导致心肌供血不足，主要症状包括胸痛（心绞痛）和心脏病发作（心肌梗死）。其诊断通常依赖于病史、临床症状、心电图、影像学检查（如冠状动脉造影）等方法。疾病的风险因素包括年龄、男性、家族历史、高LDL胆固醇、糖尿病、吸烟等。CAD的严重程度和表现可能因患者的具体情况而异，及时进入诊断和治疗过程对于改善预后至关重要。

Severe hyperthyroidism, particularly thyrotoxic crisis (thyroid storm), can lead to significant cardiovascular complications including palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, arrhythmias, heart failure, and reversible myocardial dysfunction (thyrotoxic cardiomyopathy). The increased metabolic demand, direct toxic effects of excess thyroid hormone on myocytes, and potential microvascular dysfunction can lead to elevated myocardial enzymes and ECG changes that mimic acute MI. The URTI could have precipitated the hyperthyroid decompensation or crisis.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Hyperthyroidism, Thyroid storm, Thyrotoxic cardiomyopathy, Cardiovascular complications of hyperthyroidism, Myocardial dysfunction in hyperthyroidism, Thyroid hormone effects on cardiac myocytes, Microvascular dysfunction in hyperthyroidism, Elevated myocardial enzymes, ECG changes in hyperthyroidism, Acute myocardial infarction mimicry, URTI precipitating thyroid crisis
judgment_path.info:
Hyperthyroidism, or overactive thyroid, occurs when the thyroid gland produces excessive thyroid hormones, leading to an accelerated metabolism. Common symptoms include unintended weight loss, rapid or irregular heartbeat, hand tremors, increased hunger, nervousness, sweating, changes in menstrual cycles, fatigue, and muscle weakness. Diagnosis can be challenging as symptoms may mimic other health issues. It is essential to consult a healthcare provider if experiencing significant symptoms such as unexplained weight loss or a noticeable swelling at the neck's base. Regular follow-up with the provider is often necessary after diagnosis to manage the condition effectively.

Hyperthyroidism, or hyperthyroidism disorder, is an endocrine disease caused by excessive secretion of thyroid hormones. Key clinical symptoms include high metabolic activity, cardiovascular abnormalities, and thyroid enlargement. The most common cause is Graves' disease, accounting for about 85% of cases, with other causes like toxic nodular goiter. Diagnosis involves decreased levels of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and elevated thyroid hormones (T3/T4), along with TRAb antibody testing and imaging studies. The global prevalence ranges from approximately 0.5% in iodine-sufficient regions to 10-15% in iodine-deficient areas. Treatment options include antithyroid medications, radioactive iodine therapy (which has a high cure rate), and thyroidectomy. Hyperthyroidism is linked with various systemic diseases, and post-surgery TSH levels and tumor size are critical for prognostic assessment. Recent guidelines emphasize the importance of addressing nonspecific symptoms in elderly patients and developing a tiered treatment plan.

Hyperthyroidism, or overactive thyroid, occurs when the thyroid produces excessive hormones, leading to symptoms such as rapid heart rate, weight loss despite increased appetite, anxiety, tremors, sweating, and menstrual changes. Diagnosis typically involves a physical examination, blood tests to check hormone levels, and imaging tests. Treatment options include antithyroid medications, radioactive iodine therapy, surgery, and beta-blockers to manage symptoms. It's crucial to seek medical advice if symptoms arise, as untreated hyperthyroidism can lead to serious complications like atrial fibrillation and thyroid storm.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient's rapid improvement with supportive therapy, including cardiac medications and supplements, and the normalization of myocardial enzymes, ECG ST-segment elevation, and improved cardiac function (EF to 55% within 3 days) strongly suggest a reversible cause of myocardial injury rather than irreversible ischemic necrosis from coronary occlusion. The subsequent normalization of both thyroid and cardiac function at the 6-month follow-up further solidifies the diagnosis of a reversible cardiac condition driven by severe hyperthyroidism. The initial ECG findings, initially interpreted as MI, were likely atypical manifestations of the hyperthyroid state or associated inflammation.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Hyperthyroidism and cardiac function, Myocardial enzyme normalization, ECG ST-segment elevation etiology, Ejection fraction (EF) improvement causes, Myocardial injury reversible causes, Coronary occlusion and ischemic necrosis differentiation, Hyperthyroidism-associated inflammation
judgment_path.info:
Hyperthyroidism, or overactive thyroid, occurs when the thyroid gland produces excessive thyroid hormones, leading to symptoms such as unexplained weight loss, rapid or irregular heartbeat, hand tremors, increased appetite, nervousness, sweating, changes in menstrual cycles, and an enlarged thyroid (goiter). Older adults may present with subtler symptoms, including fatigue and depression. Diagnosis can be complicated due to overlapping symptoms with other conditions. If you experience signs like unintended weight loss or rapid heartbeat, it is important to consult a healthcare provider for evaluation and potential diagnosis.

Hyperthyroidism, or hyperthyroidism, is an endocrine disorder caused by excessive secretion of thyroid hormones, leading to symptoms such as high metabolic rates, cardiovascular irregularities, and thyroid enlargement. The most common cause is Graves' disease, accounting for 85% of cases, with other types including toxic nodular goiter. Diagnosis is based on decreased thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels and elevated thyroid hormones (T3/T4), supplemented by TRAb antibody testing and imaging studies. The disease has a prevalence of about 0.5% in iodine-sufficient regions and up to 10%-15% in iodine-deficient areas. Treatment options include antithyroid medications (such as methimazole), radioactive iodine therapy, and thyroidectomy, with radioactive iodine therapy noted for its high one-time cure rate. Hyperthyroidism is significantly associated with cardiovascular diseases, gastrointestinal inflammation, and diabetes, among other multiorgan conditions, with postoperative TSH levels and tumor diameter being critical prognostic indicators. Recent guidelines stress the importance of tailored treatment approaches, especially for elderly patients.

Hyperthyroidism, or overactive thyroid, occurs when the thyroid gland produces excessive thyroid hormones, primarily due to conditions like Graves’ disease. Symptoms can manifest suddenly or gradually and may include rapid heart rate, increased appetite, weight loss, anxiety, shakiness, frequent bowel movements, increased sweating, hair loss, and menstrual changes. Diagnosis involves a physical examination, thyroid hormone blood tests, and possibly imaging tests. Effective treatments include antithyroid medications, radioactive iodine therapy, and surgery, with careful monitoring to prevent complications such as atrial fibrillation, heart failure, or thyroid storm. It’s crucial to seek medical attention if symptoms arise or worsen.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Acute inferior-posterior myocardial infarction was initially included based on ST-segment elevation on ECG and elevated troponin I levels, but it was excluded after coronary CTA revealed no stenosis or coronary abnormalities, leading to the diagnosis of acute fulminant myocarditis.

<Step 2> Acute fulminant myocarditis was confirmed by cardiac MRI findings of myocardial edema and injury in the left ventricle, elevated myocardial biomarkers, and clinical presentation following a recent viral illness, with coronary ischemia ruled out by CTA.

<Step 3> Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism was considered based on thyrotoxicosis findings (elevated T3 and T4, suppressed TSH levels) and recent exposure to iodinated contrast media used during coronary CTA, consistent with the Jod–Basedow phenomenon triggered by thyroid hormone dysregulation.
<Step 4> Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism was included after laboratory findings confirmed hyperthyroidism without any evidence of thyroid toxicity symptoms.

<Step 5> Autoimmune thyroid disease or alternate forms of thyrotoxicosis were excluded due to the absence of a goiter, normal thyroid ultrasound, and lack of clinical findings suggestive of autoimmune thyroid disorders or thyroid storm.

<Step 6> Specific histotypes of myocarditis such as eosinophilic myocarditis and giant cell myocarditis were considered unlikely due to the lack of myocardial biopsy and absence of specific corroborative findings.

<Step 7> The final diagnosis was acute fulminant myocarditis accompanied by iodine-induced hyperthyroidism, with myocarditis likely triggered by a recent viral infection and hyperthyroidism attributed to iodinated contrast exposure during coronary CTA (Jod–Basedow phenomenon).
<Step 8> Both conditions improved with supportive care and a low-iodine diet.
